title: Hot and Sour Soup

seo.imageAlt: A steaming bowl of authentic Chinese Hot and Sour Soup garnished with green onions.
seo.imageCaption: Enjoy a comforting bowl of this classic Hot and Sour Soup, perfect for a quick and flavorful meal.

description: Hot and Sour Soup is a beloved classic in Chinese cuisine, known for its tantalizing balance of tangy vinegar, fiery chili, and savory umami flavors. This warming and invigorating soup features tender tofu, earthy mushrooms, and bamboo shoots, all brought together in a rich, thickened broth. It is the perfect starter or light meal, offering a comforting and exciting culinary experience.

instructions.title: Prepare Ingredients
instructions.text: Rehydrate dried shiitake and wood ear mushrooms in hot water for 20-30 minutes. Once soft, squeeze out excess water, remove stems from shiitake, and slice both types of mushrooms thinly. Slice pork tenderloin or firm tofu into thin strips. Prepare a cornstarch slurry by mixing cornstarch with 3 tablespoons of cold water. Beat the eggs in a small bowl.

instructions.title: Simmer Broth and Main Ingredients
instructions.text: In a large pot, bring chicken or vegetable broth to a boil. Add the sliced pork or tofu, rehydrated shiitake mushrooms, wood ear mushrooms, and bamboo shoots. Reduce heat to medium-low and simmer for 5-7 minutes, allowing the flavors to meld and the ingredients to cook through.

instructions.title: Season the Soup
instructions.text: Stir in the rice vinegar, soy sauce, dark soy sauce, and white pepper. Taste and adjust seasonings as needed. This is where the hot and sour balance is achieved, so feel free to add more vinegar or white pepper to your preference.

instructions.title: Thicken the Soup
instructions.text: Slowly pour the cornstarch slurry into the simmering soup in a thin stream, stirring constantly to prevent lumps. Continue stirring until the soup thickens to your desired consistency. If it becomes too thick, add a little more broth or water.

instructions.title: Add Eggs and Finish
instructions.text: While stirring the soup gently, slowly drizzle in the beaten eggs. The eggs will form delicate ribbons as they cook in the hot broth. Remove the pot from the heat immediately after adding the eggs to prevent overcooking them.

instructions.title: Final Touches and Serve
instructions.text: Stir in the sesame oil and chili oil. Ladle the hot soup into individual bowls. Garnish generously with chopped green onions before serving. Enjoy this comforting and flavorful Hot and Sour Soup immediately.

tips: For extra depth of flavor, use homemade chicken broth. If using store-bought, opt for a low-sodium version to control the saltiness.
Adjust the amount of rice vinegar and white pepper to your personal taste. Some prefer it tangier, others spicier. For more heat, add extra chili oil or a pinch of dried chili flakes.
To make it vegetarian, ensure you use vegetable broth and firm tofu instead of pork. You can also add other vegetables like shredded carrots or spinach during the simmering stage.
article:
Unlocking the secrets of a truly remarkable Chinese classic, Hot and Sour Soup stands as a testament to the intricate balance of flavors that define Asian cuisine. This beloved dish, with its invigorating kick and comforting warmth, has captivated palates worldwide, offering a symphony of spicy, tangy, savory, and umami notes in every spoonful. Whether you’re a connoisseur of Chinese food or new to its wonders, mastering the art of this soup at home is a rewarding culinary journey. Get ready to dive into the history, ingredients, and step-by-step process of creating your own authentic Hot and Sour Soup, a dish that promises to awaken your senses.

## What is Hot and Sour Soup?

Hot and Sour Soup, known in Mandarin as *Suan La Tang*, is a quintessential Chinese soup renowned for its distinctive flavor profile: a harmonious blend of "hot" (spicy) and "sour" (tangy). Originating from the northern regions of China, particularly Sichuan and Hunan provinces, its popularity has spread globally, becoming a staple on menus from humble eateries to upscale restaurants.

The "hot" element typically comes from white pepper and, optionally, chili oil or paste, offering a different kind of warmth than just pure capsaicin heat. The "sour" is predominantly derived from rice vinegar, providing a bright, acidic counterpoint. Beyond these two defining characteristics, the soup boasts a rich umami depth from ingredients like soy sauce, sesame oil, and rehydrated shiitake mushrooms. Texturally, it's a delight, featuring slender shreds of pork, delicate strips of tofu, crunchy bamboo shoots, wood ear mushrooms, and often, beautiful ribbons of egg swirled throughout a thick, velvety broth. It's a complex, invigorating, and deeply satisfying dish that truly embodies the spirit of Chinese comfort food.

## Ingredients You Need

Crafting the perfect Hot and Sour Soup recipe begins with assembling the right ingredients. Quality matters here, as each component plays a crucial role in achieving that signature balance of flavors and textures.

*   **For the Broth Base:**
    *   6-8 cups chicken or vegetable broth (homemade is best for depth)
*   **Proteins & Fungi:**
    *   4 oz lean pork loin or tenderloin, thinly shredded (or substitute with chicken breast, shrimp, or more tofu for vegetarian)
    *   8 oz firm or extra-firm tofu, pressed and thinly shredded or cubed
    *   6-8 dried shiitake mushrooms, rehydrated, stems removed, and thinly sliced (reserve soaking liquid!)
    *   1/2 cup dried wood ear mushrooms, rehydrated and thinly sliced (optional, but adds authentic crunch)
*   **Vegetables & Aromatics:**
    *   1 cup bamboo shoots, thinly shredded
    *   2 cloves garlic, minced
    *   1 inch ginger, grated or minced
*   **Flavorings & Seasonings:**
    *   1/4 cup rice vinegar (or more, to taste)
    *   1/4 cup light soy sauce
    *   1 tablespoon dark soy sauce (for color)
    *   1-2 tablespoons white pepper (crucial for the "hot" kick)
    *   1-2 teaspoons chili oil or chili garlic paste (optional, for extra heat)
    *   1 teaspoon sesame oil
*   **Thickener & Binder:**
    *   3 tablespoons cornstarch mixed with 1/4 cup cold water (cornstarch slurry)
    *   2 large eggs, whisked
*   **Garnish:**
    *   2 green onions, thinly sliced

## Step-by-Step Instructions

Follow this Hot and Sour Soup recipe closely to create a bowl that rivals your favorite Chinese restaurant.

1.  **Preparation is Key:** Begin by rehydrating your dried shiitake and wood ear mushrooms in hot water for at least 20-30 minutes, or until soft. Once rehydrated, squeeze out excess water, remove shiitake stems, and thinly slice all mushrooms. *Crucially, reserve the shiitake soaking liquid – it's packed with umami and will be added to your broth.* Shred your pork, tofu, and bamboo shoots. Mince the garlic and ginger. Prepare your cornstarch slurry and whisk your eggs in separate bowls.
2.  **Sauté Aromatics & Pork:** In a large pot or Dutch oven, heat a tablespoon of oil over medium-high heat. Add the minced garlic and ginger, stirring for about 30 seconds until fragrant. Add the shredded pork and stir-fry until it's just cooked through and no longer pink.
3.  **Build the Broth:** Pour in the chicken or vegetable broth, along with the reserved shiitake soaking liquid (carefully leaving any sediment behind). Add the sliced shiitake mushrooms, wood ear mushrooms, and shredded bamboo shoots. Bring the soup to a gentle boil, then reduce the heat to a simmer.
4.  **Seasoning & Flavor:** Stir in the rice vinegar, light soy sauce, dark soy sauce, white pepper, and chili oil (if using). Let the soup simmer for 5-7 minutes, allowing the flavors to meld beautifully. Taste and adjust seasonings – this is where you can customize the "hot" and "sour" to your preference. Add more vinegar for tang, more white pepper for heat.
5.  **Thicken the Soup:** Give your cornstarch slurry a quick stir to ensure it's well mixed. Slowly pour the slurry into the simmering soup in a thin stream, stirring constantly, until the soup thickens to your desired consistency. You might not need all of it.
6.  **Add the Egg Ribbons:** With the soup still at a gentle simmer, slowly drizzle the whisked eggs into the soup in a thin stream, stirring gently in one direction with a fork or chopsticks. This will create beautiful, delicate egg ribbons throughout the soup.
7.  **Finishing Touches:** Remove the pot from the heat. Stir in the sesame oil. Taste one last time and adjust any final seasonings.
8.  **Garnish and Serve:** Ladle the hot soup into bowls and garnish generously with sliced green onions.

## Pro Tips for the Best Results

Achieving that authentic, restaurant-quality Hot and Sour Soup recipe requires a few insider tricks:

*   **Balance is Key:** The magic of Hot and Sour Soup lies in its balance. Don't be afraid to taste and adjust the vinegar and white pepper throughout the process. Some prefer it tangier, others spicier.
*   **The Power of White Pepper:** While chili oil adds a different kind of heat, white pepper is absolutely essential for the characteristic "hot" element of this soup. Don't skimp on it!
*   **Shiitake Soaking Liquid:** Never discard the liquid used to rehydrate dried shiitake mushrooms. It's liquid gold, packed with intense umami that will significantly deepen the soup's flavor. Just strain it to remove any grit.
*   **Perfect Egg Ribbons:** For delicate, wispy egg ribbons, ensure your soup is at a gentle simmer (not a rolling boil) and pour the whisked egg in a very slow, thin stream while gently stirring the soup in a consistent circular motion.
*   **Don't Over-Thicken:** Add the cornstarch slurry gradually. You want a velvety, slightly viscous consistency, not a gloopy one. The soup will continue to thicken slightly as it cools.
*   **Resting Time:** Like many soups, Hot and Sour Soup often tastes even better after sitting for 10-15 minutes, allowing the flavors to fully meld.

## Serving Suggestions

Hot and Sour Soup is incredibly versatile. It makes for an excellent appetizer before a larger Chinese meal, complementing dishes like General Tso's Chicken, Kung Pao Shrimp, or a simple vegetable stir-fry. It can also stand alone as a light, comforting lunch, especially when paired with a side of steamed rice or a few potstickers. For an extra flourish, consider garnishing with a sprinkle of fresh cilantro, a drizzle of extra chili oil for those who crave more heat, or even some crispy fried shallots. A cup of jasmine tea or a light lager makes for a perfect beverage pairing.

## Variations and Substitutions

One of the great things about this Hot and Sour Soup recipe is its adaptability. Feel free to experiment based on your dietary preferences and what you have on hand.

*   **Vegetarian/Vegan Version:** Easily transform this into a vegetarian or vegan delight by using vegetable broth, omitting the pork, and ensuring your tofu is ample. Skip the egg for a fully vegan option, or use a flax egg substitute for thickening.
*   **Protein Alternatives:** Instead of pork, you can use thinly sliced chicken breast, shredded duck, or even shrimp (add shrimp in the last few minutes of simmering to avoid overcooking).
*   **Spice Level:** Adjust the amount of white pepper and chili oil/paste to your personal heat tolerance. For a milder soup, reduce the chili. For a fiery kick, add more!
*   **Sourness:** Similarly, the amount of rice vinegar can be tweaked. Start with the recommended amount and add more at the end if you prefer a more pronounced tang.
*   **Additional Vegetables:** Feel free to incorporate other thinly sliced vegetables such as carrots, snow peas, or water chestnuts for added crunch and nutrition.
*   **Traditional Touches:** For an even more authentic Hot and Sour Soup recipe, seek out dried lily flowers (also known as golden needles) and include them during the broth simmering stage. They add a unique floral note and texture.

This classic Hot and Sour Soup recipe offers a delightful culinary experience, marrying bold flavors with comforting textures. By following these steps and tips, you're not just making a soup; you're creating a bowl of invigorating warmth that is deeply satisfying and endlessly customizable. So, gather your ingredients, fire up your stove, and prepare to impress your taste buds with this iconic Chinese masterpiece!
